serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled kale as a side dish is approximately 85 g (about half a cup of cooked leaves).

nutrient_source.title: Kale, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169238)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for boiled kale (drained, without salt) used as primary nutrient anchor. Singapore HPB values for energy (36 kcal), protein (2.94 g), fat (1.21 g), carbohydrate (5.3 g), sugar (1.21 g), sodium (16 mg), dietary fibre (4.0 g), calcium (150 mg), and iron (0.84 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data and are retained from the input record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin C, vitamin A, and other micronutrients are supplemented from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for boiled kale exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Kale is a non-starchy leafy vegetable with 5.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which 4.0 g is dietary fibre, yielding approximately 1.3 g available carbohydrate. GI is estimated at approximately 4 by analogy with other non-starchy boiled leafy greens (e.g., spinach, cabbage) documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 85 g serving = (4 × 1.3 × 0.85) / 100 ≈ 0.2, classified as negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (16 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (228 mg/100 g) with high dietary fibre (4.0 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled kale is highly suitable for hypertensive patients due to its very low sodium content (16 mg/100 g) and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio, consistent with DASH dietary guidelines; patients should avoid adding salt or high-sodium sauces after cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low available carbohydrate (~1.3 g/100 g net), high dietary fibre (4.0 g/100 g), and estimated GI of approximately 4
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled kale is an excellent choice for individuals with type 2 diabetes; its negligible available carbohydrate content and high soluble fibre minimise postprandial blood glucose excursions and support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.16 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (4.0 g/100 g), potassium (228 mg/100 g), and antioxidant vitamins C and E
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled kale supports cardiovascular health through its low saturated fat and cholesterol content, high fibre for LDL reduction, and potassium and antioxidant micronutrients that contribute to vascular protection; regular consumption is consistent with cardioprotective dietary patterns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in sulforaphane precursors, vitamin C (41 mg/100 g), vitamin K, calcium (150 mg/100 g), and anti-inflammatory antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled kale is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory phytonutrients and bone-protective calcium and vitamin K content; patients on warfarin anticoagulation therapy should monitor vitamin K intake consistently to avoid fluctuations in INR.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled kale is a minimally processed leafy green vegetable prepared by submerging fresh kale leaves (Brassica oleracea var. sabellica or acephala) in boiling water until tender, then draining. The result is a soft, slightly chewy vegetable with a mildly bitter, earthy flavour that mellows compared to raw kale. Boiling reduces the volume of the leaves substantially and slightly reduces heat-sensitive nutrients such as vitamin C relative to raw kale, but the food remains highly nutritious. Per 100 g, boiled kale provides approximately 36 kcal, 2.94 g protein, 1.21 g fat, 5.3 g carbohydrate (of which 4.0 g is dietary fibre and only 1.21 g is sugar), 16 mg sodium, 150 mg calcium, 0.84 mg iron, 228 mg potassium, and 41 mg vitamin C. Its very low available carbohydrate content and high fibre make it an excellent food for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes. The low sodium and favourable potassium content align with DASH dietary recommendations for hypertension. The absence of cholesterol and very low saturated fat support cardiovascular health. Kale is a cruciferous vegetable containing glucosinolates that are metabolised to sulforaphane and other bioactive compounds with anti-inflammatory and potentially anti-cancer properties. It is widely consumed globally as a side dish, in salads (when raw), soups, stews, and stir-fries. Boiled kale is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB database (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for boiled kale (FDC ID 169238). Values are consistent across both approved sources. Micronutrient values supplemented from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008) as no direct GI measurement exists for boiled kale.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced from Singapore HPB database and are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values. 2. Saturated fat (0.16 g), monounsaturated fat (0.09 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.54 g), potassium (228 mg), vitamin C (41 mg), vitamin A (53 mcg RAE), B vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169238) for boiled kale. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: kale is a plant food and contains no trans fats by nature (naturally zero). 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: kale is a plant food and contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: kale is a plant food and contains no vitamin B12 (naturally zero). 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: kale contains negligible vitamin D (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy). 7. GI estimated at approximately 4 by analogy with non-starchy boiled leafy greens per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for boiled kale is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. Boiled kale is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. Local names fields are set to null. 9. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and SNOMED CT 412061001 are used as best available matches for kale as a food substance; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used for kale. These codes should be verified against the latest ontology releases.
